claims.normalized_claim: In Terry v. Ohio (1968) the Supreme Court held that a police officer may, without a warrant or probable cause, briefly stop a person and conduct a limited pat-down of outer clothing for weapons when the officer has reasonable suspicion that criminal activity is afoot and that the person is armed and dangerous.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Warren, C.J.), holding
counterevidence: Douglas, J. dissented, arguing this fell below the probable-cause requirement.
allowed_wording: The Court ruled police can stop and frisk you on reasonable suspicion, a standard lower than probable cause.
prohibited_wording: The Court said police can search anyone for any reason.
Terry abolished the Fourth Amendment on the street.

claims.normalized_claim: Terry v. Ohio was decided on June 10, 1968 by an 8-1 vote, with the majority opinion written by Chief Justice Earl Warren.
allowed_wording: In an 8-1 decision in 1968, Chief Justice Warren wrote for the Court.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court treated a stop as a 'seizure' and a frisk as a 'search' under the Fourth Amendment, but held that this limited intrusion can be 'reasonable' on less than probable cause.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, Fourth Amendment analysis
allowed_wording: A stop is a seizure and a frisk is a search, but the Court said this limited kind can be reasonable on a lower standard.
prohibited_wording: The Court said a frisk is not a search at all.

claims.normalized_claim: The standard the Court applied — later called 'reasonable suspicion' — requires the officer to point to specific and articulable facts, not an inchoate hunch.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, 'specific and articulable facts'
allowed_wording: Reasonable suspicion means facts the officer can describe, not just a feeling.
prohibited_wording: Reasonable suspicion means the officer needs no reason at all.

claims.normalized_claim: The incident occurred in downtown Cleveland, Ohio on October 31, 1963; plainclothes Detective Martin McFadden, who had been a policeman for 39 years (35 as a detective), observed the men.
allowed_wording: A veteran Cleveland detective, Martin McFadden, was watching the street in 1963.
claims.notes: Real person by role; facts per opinion.

claims.normalized_claim: McFadden watched John W. Terry and Richard Chilton repeatedly walk past and peer into a store window — about a dozen trips in all, roughly five or six each — suspected they were 'casing a job, a stick-up,' stopped and patted them down, and recovered a pistol from each.
allowed_wording: The two men cased a store window about a dozen times; the detective patted them down and found a gun on each.
prohibited_wording: The men were caught committing a robbery.
The detective saw them commit a crime.
claims.notes: Real people charged accurately; suspicion, not a completed crime. A third companion (Katz) was frisked but unarmed; omitted for concision (not contradicted).

claims.normalized_claim: Terry was charged with carrying a concealed weapon, and the Supreme Court upheld the admission of the pistol against him.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, procedural posture and holding
allowed_wording: Terry was charged with carrying a concealed weapon and the Court let the gun stand as evidence.

claims.normalized_claim: Justice William O. Douglas was the sole dissenter, warning that giving an officer power to seize and search on less than probable cause gave police greater authority than a magistrate and was 'a long step down the totalitarian path.'
allowed_wording: Justice Douglas dissented alone, warning it gave police more power than a judge.
claims.notes: Dissent (balance); short quote is public domain.

claims.normalized_claim: The 'Terry stop' became one of the most common police-citizen encounters in the United States, and because 'reasonable suspicion' depends on judgment, its application later became the center of national debate over stop-and-frisk practices.
counterevidence: The 1968 ruling itself required specific facts and limited the frisk to weapons; later controversy concerns application, not the holding.
allowed_wording: Terry stops became routine, and how 'reasonable suspicion' is applied later drove a national argument.
prohibited_wording: The Supreme Court endorsed racial profiling.
claims.notes: Legacy/debate kept neutral; B-grade context, framed as application not holding.

findings: Durable content hashes captured for all 2 sources (2026-06-20).
